coaching.coaching: Pin down canonical labels key contract at the boundary with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: canonical labels key contract at the boundary

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for duplicate reporter-resolver across core and adapter — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: duplicate reporter-resolver across core and adapter

coaching.coaching: Plan how out-of-scope consumer of demoted adapter helpers will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.

coaching.subject: per-ticket mutation of shared mapper instance

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `_map_jira_to_local_fields` emits a `labels: list[str]` canonical key (guarded on `'labels' in jira_fields`, per §7 partial-tolerance) and that `_diff_labels` / `_diff_status_annotation_labels` in `outbound_differ.py` consume `canonical_remote.get('labels', [])` rather than `jira_fields.get('labels')`, closing the Step 4c label-canonicalization gap.
evidence: Plan §4c: 'The Jira map_remote_to_local output gains labels: list[str] (a pass-through translation of fields.labels), and _diff_labels consumes the canonical list'
outbound_differ.py:320: `for label in (jira_fields.get('labels') or [])` — reads raw vendor dict, not canonical_remote
outbound_differ.py:768: `jira_labels=list(jira_fields.get('labels') or [])` — same for annotation labels
inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields: no 'labels' key is emitted in the output dict (labels are consumed internally only for rebar-status: processing)
Testing section names 'label add/remove decisions via the canonical labels list' as a characterization case — but the mechanism to provide that list (canonical output key) is absent
findings.finding: Step 4c states that `_diff_labels` will consume a canonical `labels: list[str]` key from `map_remote_to_local` output, but the implementation keeps `_diff_labels` reading raw `jira_fields.get('labels')` directly (outbound_differ.py:320 and :768), and `_map_jira_to_local_fields` emits no `labels` canonical key. The label comparison path in core still reads the raw vendor snapshot shape — the stated §4c mechanism is incomplete, leaving `outbound_differ.py` reading `jira_fields.get('labels')` (a vendor key) contrary to the seam principle the plan enforces everywhere else.
findings.location: Step 4c ('Labels canonicalize here too') / outbound_differ.py:320, :762, :768 / inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields
scenarios: A second backend whose snapshot uses a different key for labels (e.g. 'tags' instead of 'labels') would break _diff_labels without any core change, defeating the seam
The neutrality AST gate (AC2 denylist for vendor key literals) may not catch 'labels' since it is not listed in the denylist — so the violation is untested by the stated gate
findings.suggested_fix: In `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields`, add `if 'labels' in jira_fields: out['labels'] = list(jira_fields.get('labels') or [])`. Update `_diff_labels` signature to accept `list[str]` instead of the raw `jira_fields` dict, and update both call sites (line 762: pass `canonical_remote.get('labels', [])`, line 768: same). Update `_diff_status_annotation_labels` call to also use the canonical list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether Step 4c (labels canonicalization) is intentionally deferred or was silently dropped: `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` does not emit a `labels: list[str]` canonical key and `_diff_labels` still reads raw `jira_fields.get('labels')` — if 4c is in scope, add `labels` emission to the inbound mapper and update `_diff_labels` to accept a canonical list instead of the raw vendor dict.
evidence: Plan Step 4c: 'The Jira map_remote_to_local output gains labels: list[str] (a pass-through translation of fields.labels), and _diff_labels consumes the canonical list'

inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields (lines 168-232): output dict has keys title, description, ticket_type, priority, assignee, assignee_identity, reporter_identity, remote_parent_id, status — no 'labels' key emitted
outbound_differ._diff_labels (lines 296-338): signature is _diff_labels(ticket, jira_fields, intent_set) and reads jira_fields.get('labels') at line 320 — still consuming raw vendor shape
_diff_labels called at outbound_differ.py:762 as _diff_labels(ticket, jira_fields, intent_set) — jira_fields is the raw vendor snapshot entry, not a canonical dict
findings.finding: Step 4c asserts that `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local` output gains a `labels: list[str]` canonical key and that `_diff_labels` is updated to consume that canonical list rather than the raw `jira_fields` dict. Probing the code refutes both halves: `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` does NOT emit a `labels` key (the function reads `jira_fields.get('labels')` internally only for status-annotation detection but does not place it in the output dict), and `_diff_labels` in `outbound_differ.py` still accepts `jira_fields: dict[str, Any]` and reads `jira_fields.get('labels')` directly (line 320), leaving the label-comparison path reading raw vendor shape rather than the canonical output.
scenarios: Label canonicalization (step 4c) was not implemented: _diff_labels still reads raw Jira snapshot shape, contradicting AC2's vendor-key denylist and the canonical-comparison principle the plan claims to enforce end-to-end.
The testing section calls out 'label add/remove decisions via the canonical labels list' as a characterization case, but there is no canonical labels list in the mapper output to test against.
findings.suggested_fix: In `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields`, add `if 'labels' in jira_fields: out['labels'] = list(jira_fields.get('labels') or [])`. Update `_diff_labels` signature to accept `canonical_remote: dict[str, Any]` (or a `labels: list[str]`) instead of `jira_fields`, and read `canonical_remote.get('labels', [])`. Update the call site at `outbound_differ.py:762` accordingly. If 4c is intentionally out of scope, remove it from the plan's Step 4c and Testing sections and note it as a follow-on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Step 4c (labels canonicalized via `InboundMapper`) and Step 9/ADR amendment (labels remain raw-snapshot reads): either remove Step 4c and update the Testing/AC bullet to drop the 'canonical labels list' claim, or explicitly add `labels` to `_map_jira_to_local_fields` output and wire `_diff_labels` to consume it (and update the ADR corollary accordingly).
evidence: Step 4c: '_diff_labels (outbound_differ.py:755) today reads the raw jira_fields.get("labels"). The Jira map_remote_to_local output gains labels: list[str] (a pass-through translation of fields.labels), and _diff_labels consumes the canonical list'
ADR §(d) corollary (landed text, line 154-155): 'Labels/comments/links remain read from the raw snapshot by their own capability-diff paths — only the field path is canonical.'
outbound_differ.py:320: 'for label in (jira_fields.get("labels") or [])' — still reading raw vendor dict
outbound_differ.py:768: 'jira_labels=list(jira_fields.get("labels") or [])' — still reading raw vendor dict
inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields: no 'labels' key is emitted in the output dict
findings.finding: Step 4c claims `_diff_labels` will consume a canonical `labels: list[str]` key emitted by `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local`, but the ADR §(d) amendment (Step 9 / ADR text line 154-155) explicitly states the opposite: 'Labels/comments/links remain read from the raw snapshot by their own capability-diff paths — only the field path is canonical.' The actual landed code confirms the contradiction: `_map_jira_to_local_fields` emits no `labels` canonical key, and `_diff_labels` / `_diff_status_annotation_labels` in `outbound_differ.py` still read `jira_fields.get('labels')` from the raw vendor dict.
findings.location: Step 4c (label canonicalization) vs. Step 9 / ADR amendment text; outbound_differ.py:320, outbound_differ.py:768; inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields
scenarios: A future reader of the plan implements Step 4c as written, adding a `labels` canonical key to `map_remote_to_local` and wiring `_diff_labels` to consume it — then discovers the ADR says the opposite and the landed code doesn't do it, causing confusion about the true scope boundary.
The AC for labels testing ('label add/remove decisions via the canonical labels list') will fail if taken literally, since the labels path is NOT canonicalized.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove Step 4c entirely (labels stay vendor-shaped per the ADR) and update the Testing bullet 'label add/remove decisions via the canonical labels list' to 'label add/remove decisions via the raw jira_fields labels list (unchanged)' — consistent with the ADR and the landed code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the adapter-side `_resolve_reporter_account_id` (`adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:333-343`) is retired (deleted or made to delegate to the core copy) when `_diff_fields` is demoted in step 8, to avoid maintaining two byte-identical implementations.
evidence: outbound_field_diff.py lines 83-93: `def _resolve_reporter_account_id(local_reporter: Any) -> str | None:` calling `_identity.jira_account_id(local_reporter)`
adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py lines 333-343: identical `def _resolve_reporter_account_id(local_reporter: Any) -> str | None:` calling `_identity.jira_account_id(local_reporter)`
Step 8 lists `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, `_assignee_matches`, `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` as becoming adapter-internal but does not mention `_resolve_reporter_account_id`
findings.finding: `_resolve_reporter_account_id` is implemented identically in both the new core module `outbound_field_diff.py` (lines 83-93) and the existing adapter module `adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py` (lines 333-343) — same body, same `rebar._commands.identity.jira_account_id` call, same exception swallow — while the plan's step 8 only retires `_diff_fields`/`_extract_jira_field`/`_assignee_matches`/`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` without calling for consolidation or removal of the duplicated reporter helper.
scenarios: If the resolution logic (e.g. identity-seam import path or exception handling) is changed in one copy, the other silently diverges — reporter diffing behaves differently in the old vs new path.
The adapter-side copy is now dead code within `_diff_fields`, which the plan marks as demoted — but it is not explicitly removed, so both bodies persist.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to step 8: retire `adapters/jira/outbound_fields._resolve_reporter_account_id` alongside `_diff_fields` (delete or have it call the core helper), so only one authoritative copy exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reconcile_check.py` to the consumer enumeration in Step 8 with a note that it intentionally retains its lazy-`_load_sibling` usage of `_extract_jira_field`/`_assignee_matches` from `adapters/jira/outbound_fields` (the same exemption documented in `test_backend_neutrality.py:94-99`).
evidence: Step 8: '_diff_fields / _extract_jira_field / _assignee_matches / _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE become adapter-internal' — plan does not mention reconcile_check.py as a consumer that bypasses this via lazy load
reconcile_check.py:70: '_load_sibling("outbound_fields", "adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py")' — lazy-loads the same symbols
reconcile_check.py:236: 'matches = of._assignee_matches(...)'
reconcile_check.py:241: 'jira_text = of._extract_jira_field(jira_issue, "description")'
test_backend_neutrality.py:94-99: exemption comment exists in test but not in plan
findings.finding: `reconcile_check.py` is an out-of-scope consumer that lazy-loads `adapters/jira/outbound_fields._extract_jira_field` and `._assignee_matches` directly (via `_load_sibling('outbound_fields', 'adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py')` at `reconcile_check.py:70`), yet the plan's Scope section and the consumer enumeration in Step 8 do not mention it. The `test_backend_neutrality.py` comment (line 94-99) deliberately exempts it, but this exemption decision is absent from the plan body, leaving an unenumerated out-of-scope dependency that a reader of the plan alone cannot discover.
findings.location: Step 8 / Scope section; reconcile_check.py:61-70, reconcile_check.py:236, reconcile_check.py:241, reconcile_check.py:247
scenarios: A plan-only reader implementing Step 8 may inadvertently change `_extract_jira_field` / `_assignee_matches` signatures or remove them, breaking `reconcile_check.py` which is not enumerated as a consumer.
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 8, add: 'Note: `reconcile_check.py` intentionally retains its lazy `_load_sibling("outbound_fields", "adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py")` usage of `_extract_jira_field` and `_assignee_matches` — routing these internals through the port would over-expose vendor internals for no behavioural gain (see `test_backend_neutrality.py:94`). Its function-nested load is not a module-level adapter import and does not violate the AST gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (§4b or a remediation note) that `resolve_assignee`'s live account resolver is injected as a mutable `_assignee_resolver` attribute on the shared mapper instance before each ticket diff, and that the try/except at outbound_field_diff.py:199-203 is the intentional fallback for mappers that don't support attribute injection.
evidence: Plan §4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool] — parameterized ONLY on the canonical assignee_identity shape above (never on jira_fields)'
outbound_field_diff.py:197-203: `outbound_mapper._assignee_resolver = (lambda lv: assignee_resolver(lv, jira_key))` inside a try/except that silently passes on AttributeError/TypeError
adapters/jira/backend.py:103: `resolver = getattr(self, '_assignee_resolver', None)` — the live resolver is fetched via attribute, not constructor injection
The shared _JiraOutbound instance is created once at JiraBackend.__init__ and reused for all tickets in a pass; mutable attribute injection per ticket is the actual mechanism
findings.finding: The `_assignee_resolver` injection mechanism — mutating the shared `_JiraOutbound` instance attribute `outbound_mapper._assignee_resolver` per ticket (outbound_field_diff.py:199) — is undocumented in the plan, which describes `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity)` as if it were self-contained. The plan (§4b) says the resolver is 'parameterized ONLY on the canonical assignee_identity shape', but in reality it also captures `jira_key` from the outer closure and is injected as a mutable side-channel on the shared mapper object. This works correctly for sequential single-threaded execution but the plan does not acknowledge the shared-state mutation or the try/except fallback that silently discards injection errors.
findings.location: Step 4b ('assignee resolver and sentinel keep their exact payload contract, re-homed §(d)-legally') / outbound_field_diff.py:197-203 / adapters/jira/backend.py _JiraOutbound.resolve_assignee
scenarios: If a mapper implementation forbids attribute injection (e.g. a __slots__ class or a frozen dataclass), the try/except silently swallows the error and resolve_assignee falls back to the no-resolver path — the plan does not say this is the intended fallback for a protocol-compliant mapper that simply doesn't support attribute injection

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in AC2 (or a plan note) whether `"parent"` should be included in the outbound_differ.py denylist, and document the local field name for the parent relationship so the executor can distinguish vendor reads from local-vocab references.
evidence: AC2: 'contains none of the vendor payload key literals "accountId", "emailAddress", "displayName", "issuetype", "issuelinks", "inwardIssue", "outwardIssue"' — "parent" is absent from this list
Step 4: 'remote_parent_id: str|None (Jira: fields.parent.key)' — establishes parent.key as a vendor shape to be replaced

Step 7: 'the outbound differs suppression/conflict path applies inbound_mapper.map_remote_to_local ... vendor suppression helpers ... replaced by neutral canonical-name equivalents' — implies no raw 'parent' key reads survive in outbound_differ.py
findings.finding: AC2's vendor-key denylist omits `"parent"` — the plan explicitly replaces `fields.parent.key` reads with the canonical key `remote_parent_id` (Step 4, Step 7), so `"parent"` as a raw Jira key literal in `outbound_differ.py` would be a seam violation, yet it is not listed in the denylist. The plan also does not state whether `"parent"` is reserved as a local field name, leaving an executor unable to determine without clarification whether to include it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 denylist; Step 4 / Step 7
scenarios: An executor adds the denylist test without `"parent"`, leaves a `snapshot_entry["parent"]` read in the neutral differ, and the AC2 test passes (false-negative) — the seam violation goes undetected.
An executor adds `"parent"` to the denylist but the local ticket model uses a field also called `parent`, breaking legitimate local-vocab references.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"parent"` to the AC2 denylist and add a plan note: 'The local field name for parent is `<local_name>` (e.g. `parent_key`); only the raw Jira `"parent"` key is banned from outbound_differ.py — local-vocab references are permitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `_backend.py` scope note for OutboundMapper Protocol additions: `map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]` and `resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]`.
evidence: Scope bullet: '_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' — OutboundMapper additions absent
Step 3: 'a NEW OutboundMapper method map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]'
Step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]'
The OutboundMapper Protocol definition is not assigned to any scope file; `adapters/jira/backend.py (delegate surface)` covers only the Jira implementation, not the Protocol.
findings.finding: The `_backend.py` scope bullet covers only InboundMapper Protocol additions; the two new OutboundMapper Protocol methods (`map_fields_to_remote` from Step 3 and `resolve_assignee` from Step 4b) are not listed in any scope bullet, creating a gap between the implementation instructions and the declared change surface.
scenarios: An executor updates the Jira adapter implementation but forgets to extend the OutboundMapper Protocol in `_backend.py`; `make typecheck` would eventually surface this, but the scope mismatch could cause unnecessary back-and-forth during implementation.
A future reviewer inspecting the scope list for `_backend.py` sees only InboundMapper and does not realize OutboundMapper was also extended — the change surface is under-documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the `_backend.py` scope bullet to: '`_backend.py` (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id; OutboundMapper Protocol extended with `map_fields_to_remote` and `resolve_assignee`).'}

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope file path for the Jira inbound mapper: either change `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` to `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py` (to match Step 4's `adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py:141-184`), or add a clarifying note if both files are intentionally modified.
evidence: Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: aliases, parent remote id, reporter aliases)'

Approach Step 4: 'implemented in the vendor mapper `inbound_fields.py:141-184` / delegate `adapters/jira/backend.py:43-47`' — the `adapters/jira/` path prefix is implicit from context but absent in the Scope entry
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` as the file to receive the new canonical alias keys (assignee_identity, remote_parent_id, reporter_identity), but the Approach (Step 4) locates the Jira inbound mapper at `adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py:141-184` — a different path that includes the `adapters/jira/` segment. An implementer following the Scope would modify the wrong file.
scenarios: Implementer edits the core `rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py` (which may not exist or may be a different module) instead of `adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py`, leaving the Jira mapper unchanged.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Scope entry with `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py (Jira mapper: assignee_identity, remote_parent_id, reporter_identity aliases; partial-key presence guard)` to match the Approach reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the backend-neutrality AST/type-check gate) that explicitly verifies `map_fields_to_remote` is declared on the `OutboundMapper` Protocol in `_backend.py` and that `FakeBackend` in the neutrality test implements it.
evidence: Step 3: 'a NEW `OutboundMapper` method `map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]`'
AC runtime proof: 'the update path builds its payload exclusively via the injected `OutboundMapper` (mirroring `test_outbound_differ_uses_injected_mapper`)'
No AC requires a static check or Protocol-signature test for `map_fields_to_remote`.
findings.finding: Step 3 introduces a new `OutboundMapper` Protocol method `map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]` as a required deliverable, but no AC verifies that the `OutboundMapper` Protocol/interface was actually extended with this method. The 'runtime proof' AC (spy InboundMapper / FakeBackend test) only verifies that the update path calls the injected OutboundMapper, not that the Protocol contract itself names `map_fields_to_remote`.
findings.location: Approach Step 3 vs Acceptance Criteria (runtime-proof AC)
scenarios: The Protocol definition is not updated (only the concrete Jira implementation gains the method), causing type-checking to miss future implementations that omit it; or a FakeBackend in tests doesn't implement `map_fields_to_remote` and the spy test passes vacuously.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit expand-contract note: identify all existing OutboundMapper implementations (Jira + test doubles), confirm they are updated atomically in the same change, and state the back-out procedure (revert Gerrit change 931) if post-merge failures are detected.
evidence: Step 3: 'OutboundMapper gains `map_fields_to_remote`'
Step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains `resolve_assignee`'
The plan names NO existing OutboundMapper implementations besides the Jira one, and provides no expand-contract or rollback path for the Protocol extension.
'make lint && make typecheck && make test' green is the only gate — this catches the break but does not constitute a rollback plan.
findings.finding: Adding `map_fields_to_remote` and `resolve_assignee` as new required methods to the `OutboundMapper` Protocol is a breaking change for any existing `OutboundMapper` implementations (the Jira implementation and any FakeBackend/test doubles). The plan acknowledges the Jira implementation change but provides no expand-contract sequencing, deprecation window, or rollback path for existing implementations that would fail a `mypy`/Protocol-compliance check after the change.
findings.location: Approach Step 3 (map_fields_to_remote), Step 4b (resolve_assignee); no corresponding rollback or expand-contract discussion anywhere in the plan.
scenarios: A second OutboundMapper implementation (e.g. a GitHub backend stub, a test double) that was compliant before the change fails Protocol compliance immediately with no migration path; if the change is reverted, the differ reverts to vendor-shaped comparison.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (via grep/AST scan across the full repo, not just the files in Scope) that the only importers of `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` are `outbound_differ.py` and the named test re-exports, and add a one-line revert reference (Gerrit change 931) as the explicit rollback path in the plan.
evidence: Step 8: '_diff_fields / _extract_jira_field / _assignee_matches / _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE become adapter-internal; the outbound_differ.py:39-43 imports are deleted and the test-suite re-exports migrate to adapters.jira.outbound_fields'
No statement enumerating all import sites beyond `outbound_differ.py` and the test suite.
No rollback path named for this symbol removal.
findings.finding: Step 8 demotes `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` from their current import surface (currently importable from `adapters.jira.outbound_fields` via `outbound_differ.py:39-41`) to adapter-internal, acknowledging only test re-export migration. No rollback path is named for external callers that import these symbols at their current locations, and the plan does not enumerate all import sites.
findings.location: Approach Step 8; Acceptance Criteria (AST import gate)
scenarios: An unscanned caller (e.g. a debugging script, an integration test, a downstream tool) imports `_diff_fields` from its current location and breaks silently; the change is already merged (Gerrit 931) with no back-out plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `_backend.py` Scope entry (and implementation) to explicitly add `map_fields_to_remote` and `resolve_assignee` to the `OutboundMapper` Protocol with typed signatures and docstring contracts (mirroring the InboundMapper treatment for the new canonical keys).
evidence: Step 3: 'a NEW OutboundMapper method map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]'
Step 4b: 'OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]'
Scope: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id)' — OutboundMapper Protocol update absent
Step 4: 'documented on the InboundMapper Protocol docstring in _backend.py' — no analogous statement for OutboundMapper
findings.finding: The plan adds two new port methods to `OutboundMapper` — `map_fields_to_remote(local_fields)` and `resolve_assignee(local_value, remote_identity)` — but the Scope entry for `_backend.py` says only 'InboundMapper contract note: canonical output incl. identity aliases + parent remote id'. There is no explicit statement that the `OutboundMapper` Protocol in `_backend.py` is updated to formally capture these new methods and their contracts (parameter types, return tuple semantics, authoritative/is_account_id flags). Without Protocol-level documentation, implementors of a second backend have no formal contract to implement against, the coupling mitigation the port boundary provides is incomplete, and the non-trivial 3-tuple return of `resolve_assignee` is undiscoverable.
scenarios: A developer implementing a GitHub or other backend must discover `map_fields_to_remote` and `resolve_assignee` from reading the Jira adapter rather than from the Protocol definition — defeating the port abstraction.

The 3-tuple `(payload_value, authoritative, is_account_id)` contract of `resolve_assignee` is described only in prose in Step 4b; without a Protocol stub, type-checkers and future implementors have no machine-checkable contract.
A backend-neutrality test that checks all Protocol methods are implemented will silently miss the new methods if the Protocol itself is not updated.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, change the `_backend.py` entry to: '`_backend.py` (InboundMapper Protocol: new canonical output keys; OutboundMapper Protocol: add `map_fields_to_remote(local_fields: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]` and `resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None) -> tuple[Any, bool, bool]` with docstrings covering the 3-tuple semantics, authoritative flag, and is_account_id sentinel)'. Add a corresponding AC bullet: 'The OutboundMapper Protocol in `_backend.py` includes typed stubs for `map_fields_to_remote` and `resolve_assignee` — verified by mypy on the Protocol class.'

findings.finding: Step 4b proposes `OutboundMapper.resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None)` — a signature that drops `jira_key`. But the current resolver `_resolve_assignee_account_id` uses `jira_key` in the `client.validate_assignee_exists(assignee, issue_key=jira_key)` call (outbound_assignee.py:108), which is the fallback path (resolution path 3) when neither the identity fast-path nor `/user/search` succeeds. The plan is silent on how `jira_key` flows to `validate_assignee_exists` in the new `resolve_assignee` method, creating an unspecified behavioral gap on the legacy string-match fallback path.
findings.location: Step 4b ('OutboundMapper gains resolve_assignee(local_value: str, remote_identity: dict | None)')

findings.finding: Step 4 asserts the new canonical alias keys are 'implemented in the vendor mapper inbound_fields.py:141-184', but there is no adapters/jira/inbound_fields.py — the file is the core module rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py. This mislabels a core file as a vendor mapper, contradicting both the Scope list and ADR 0035 §(d) (an inbound mapper that lives in core should not be described as a vendor mapper).
findings.location: Step 4 ('implemented in the vendor mapper inbound_fields.py:141-184')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'implemented in the vendor mapper `inbound_fields.py:141-184`' with 'implemented in the core inbound fields module `rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py:141-184`'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
